title: QueueService.handleWaitingLocal: Bamboo-busy and getRunningBuilds-error paths untested
description: Lines 391-392 contain two important branches with no direct tests: (a) `getRunningBuilds` returns an error (network/auth failure) — the entry must stay WAITING_LOCAL and not attempt a trigger; (b) `getRunningBuilds` returns a non-empty list (Bamboo has active builds) — the entry must stay WAITING_LOCAL. Neither scenario is covered. Without these tests, a regression that triggered builds when Bamboo is busy or when the connectivity check failed would not be caught.
suggestedFix: Add two tests in QueueServiceTest driving `pollOnce()` with a WAITING_LOCAL entry: (1) mock `getRunningBuilds` to return `ToolResult(isError=true)` and assert the entry stays WAITING_LOCAL; (2) mock `getRunningBuilds` to return a non-empty list (one running build key) and assert the entry stays WAITING_LOCAL and `triggerBuild` is never called (`coVerify(exactly=0) { bambooService.triggerBuild(any(), any(), any()) }`).
verdictReasoning: Confirmed: all three mock stubs for getRunningBuilds in QueueServiceTest (lines 567-570, 611-614, 700-703) return ToolResult.success(data = emptyList()). The two branches at QueueService.kt:392 — (a) runningResult.isError == true (network/auth failure, entry must stay WAITING_LOCAL), and (b) runningResult.data!!.isNotEmpty() (Bamboo has active builds, entry must stay WAITING_LOCAL) — are both untested. Given that handleWaitingLocal is the core auto-trigger decision path, a regression that triggers builds when Bamboo is busy or when connectivity fails would directly impact users in normal use. Severity is High as claimed.
recommendedAction: Add two tests to QueueServiceTest: one mocking getRunningBuilds to return isError=true and asserting the entry stays WAITING_LOCAL; one mocking it to return a non-empty list and asserting the entry stays WAITING_LOCAL with coVerify(exactly=0) { bambooService.triggerBuild(any(), any(), any()) }.

title: QueueService.cancel(): cancelBuild invocation never verified for QUEUED_ON_BAMBOO entries
description: Lines 213-215 show that `cancel()` calls `bambooService.cancelBuild(resultKey)` only when the entry is `QUEUED_ON_BAMBOO` and has a non-null `bambooResultKey`. The existing test only cancels a `WAITING_LOCAL` entry. There is no test that (a) seeds an entry already in `QUEUED_ON_BAMBOO` state with a `bambooResultKey` set, cancels it, and verifies `cancelBuild` is invoked; or (b) cancels a `WAITING_LOCAL` entry and verifies `cancelBuild` is NOT invoked.
suggestedFix: Add a test that seeds a `QUEUED_ON_BAMBOO` entry with `bambooResultKey = "PROJ-AUTO-500"` directly in `_stateFlow` (or via `pollOnce` after a successful trigger), calls `service.cancel("q-1")`, and then uses `coVerify(exactly=1) { bambooService.cancelBuild("PROJ-AUTO-500") }`. Add a complementary test for a `WAITING_LOCAL` entry that verifies `coVerify(exactly=0) { bambooService.cancelBuild(any()) }`.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed: no test in QueueServiceTest exercises the cancelBuild path. The existing cancel test (lines 150-158) seeds a WAITING_LOCAL entry, never a QUEUED_ON_BAMBOO entry with a bambooResultKey set. The code path at QueueService.kt:212-215 — where cancelBuild(resultKey) is called only when entry.status == QUEUED_ON_BAMBOO and bambooResultKey != null — is unverified. A regression could silently drop the Bamboo-side cancel without any test catching it. High severity confirmed: cancelling a queued Bamboo build without actually calling cancelBuild would leave orphaned builds on the CI server.
recommendedAction: Add a test that seeds a QUEUED_ON_BAMBOO entry with bambooResultKey set, calls service.cancel(), and uses coVerify(exactly=1) { bambooService.cancelBuild('PROJ-AUTO-500') }. Add a complementary test for WAITING_LOCAL entry verifying coVerify(exactly=0) { bambooService.cancelBuild(any()) }.

title: QueueService.restoreFromPersistence() has no test: crash-recovery path entirely uncovered
description: Lines 501-511 implement `restoreFromPersistence()`, which reads active entries from SQLite and re-populates `_stateFlow`, then starts the poll loop if there is live work. This is the crash-recovery path invoked by `QueueRecoveryStartupActivity` on every IDE start. No test calls this method and verifies that (a) `stateFlow` is repopulated from the persistence layer, or (b) polling starts when non-terminal entries are found. A regression here silently orphans all pre-restart queue entries.
suggestedFix: Add a test that pre-seeds the real `TagHistoryService` (with temp DB) with two `WAITING_LOCAL` entries via `saveQueueEntry`, constructs a `QueueService` with `autoTriggerEnabled=false`, calls `service.restoreFromPersistence()`, awaits the state via `awaitState { service.stateFlow.value.size == 2 }`, and asserts both entries are present with `WAITING_LOCAL` status. Also verify that an empty DB leaves `stateFlow` empty and no polling starts.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed: grep across all test files finds only one mention of restoreFromPersistence in a comment (QueueServiceTest.kt:48). No test calls service.restoreFromPersistence() and verifies stateFlow is repopulated. The method at QueueService.kt:501-512 is the entire crash-recovery path invoked by QueueRecoveryStartupActivity on every IDE start. A regression silently orphaning all pre-restart queue entries would be invisible to the test suite. Severity is High as claimed — crash recovery is invoked on every IDE start and its correctness is entirely unverified.
recommendedAction: Add a test that pre-seeds TagHistoryService with WAITING_LOCAL entries via saveQueueEntry, constructs a QueueService, calls service.restoreFromPersistence(), awaits via awaitState, and asserts both the stateFlow size and that polling started. Also test the empty-DB case.

title: QueueService.triggerNow() error path uncovered — failure result forwarding untested
description: Lines 465-498 show `doTrigger` (called by `triggerNow`) returns a forwarded `ToolResult` with `isError=true` and propagated `payload` when `bambooService.triggerBuild` fails. Only the success path is tested (the single `triggerNow bypasses queue and triggers immediately` test mocks a successful response). The failure path — including that `isError=true`, that `payload` (ErrorType) is forwarded correctly, and that no state mutation occurs — is not tested.
suggestedFix: Add a test that stubs `bambooService.triggerBuild` to return an error result with `ErrorType.AUTH_FAILED` payload, calls `service.triggerNow(entry)`, asserts `result.isError == true`, `result.data == ""`, and that the payload is `ErrorType.AUTH_FAILED`. Also verify that `_stateFlow.value` is unchanged (no entry mutation when triggerNow is called stand-alone).
verdictReasoning: Confirmed: QueueServiceTest line 217-232 has exactly one triggerNow test, which mocks a successful triggerBuild response and asserts assertFalse(result.isError). The error return path in doTrigger (QueueService.kt:492-498) — where isError=true and payload are forwarded from the bambooService.triggerBuild result — is untested via triggerNow. Note that the failure path through pollOnce/handleWaitingLocal is partially covered by the FAILED_TO_TRIGGER test (lines 605-646), but that goes through handleWaitingLocal, not triggerNow directly. The triggerNow error-return contract (that result.isError is propagated correctly, payload is forwarded, and no stateFlow mutation occurs) is not verified. Severity is Medium as the happy path is covered and the error path is partially exercised via the poll loop.
recommendedAction: Add a test that stubs bambooService.triggerBuild to return isError=true with ErrorType.AUTH_FAILED payload, calls service.triggerNow(entry), asserts result.isError == true, result.data == empty string, and payload == ErrorType.AUTH_FAILED.

title: TagHistoryService.updateQueueEntryStatus errorMessage round-trip not tested
description: Lines 199-232 show `updateQueueEntryStatus` accepts an `errorMessage: String?` parameter and writes it to the `error_message` column. The only existing test (line 111 in TagHistoryServiceTest) calls this method with `bambooResultKey` set but no `errorMessage`, and only checks `status` and `bambooResultKey`. The path where `errorMessage` is non-null (the `FAILED_TO_TRIGGER` flow writes `triggerResult.summary` as the errorMessage) is never round-tripped through SQLite.
evidence:
UPDATE queue_entries SET status = ?, updated_at = ?, error_message = ?
WHERE id = ?
suggestedFix: Add a test that saves a `WAITING_LOCAL` entry, calls `service.updateQueueEntryStatus("q-1", QueueEntryStatus.FAILED_TO_TRIGGER, errorMessage = "Bamboo 401: token expired")`, then reads the DB via `getActiveQueueEntries()` — but since FAILED_TO_TRIGGER is terminal, probe SQLite directly (as the `dismiss` test does) and assert the `error_message` column equals `"Bamboo 401: token expired"`.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed: TagHistoryServiceTest has only one updateQueueEntryStatus test at line 111-126, which calls service.updateQueueEntryStatus('q-1', QueueEntryStatus.RUNNING, bambooResultKey = 'PROJ-AUTO-849') with no errorMessage argument, and only checks status and bambooResultKey. The code at TagHistoryService.kt:199-232 has two SQL UPDATE branches: one with bambooResultKey (lines 208-218) and one without (lines 220-229). Both write errorMessage. The FAILED_TO_TRIGGER flow in QueueService writes errorMessage = triggerResult.summary (line 416-418). Neither branch's errorMessage write is covered by a round-trip SQLite test. Severity Medium is appropriate — this is a gap in the persistence contract test coverage but the code path is indirectly exercised by the FAILED_TO_TRIGGER test in QueueServiceTest (which checks the in-memory entry's errorMessage but not the SQLite column).
recommendedAction: Add a TagHistoryServiceTest that saves a WAITING_LOCAL entry, calls updateQueueEntryStatus with errorMessage = 'Bamboo 401: token expired', then probes the SQLite row directly via JDBC and asserts the error_message column equals the expected string.

title: QueueService.handleWaitingLocal: second-in-queue entry is not skipped (ordering contract untested)
description: Lines 383-387 implement the oldest-entry-first ordering: only the first `WAITING_LOCAL` entry for a given `suitePlanKey` may trigger; subsequent entries must be skipped (`if (oldestWaiting?.id != entry.id) return entry`). No test verifies this contract. A regression that removes this guard would cause all WAITING_LOCAL entries for the same suite to fire simultaneously against Bamboo.
suggestedFix: Add a test that enqueues two entries for the same `suitePlanKey` (q-1, q-2), mocks `getRunningBuilds` to return empty, mocks `triggerBuild` to succeed, calls `testService.pollOnce()`, and then asserts: only q-1 transitioned to `QUEUED_ON_BAMBOO`; q-2 remains `WAITING_LOCAL`; `coVerify(exactly=1) { bambooService.triggerBuild(any(), any(), any()) }`.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. QueueService.kt lines 383-387 implement the oldest-entry-first guard: only the entry whose id matches the firstOrNull WAITING_LOCAL entry for the same suitePlanKey may proceed to trigger. Removal of this guard would cause concurrent triggering for all queued entries of the same suite. Reviewing QueueServiceTest.kt, the tests that exercise pollOnce() (e.g. 'transient triggerBuild error', 'FAILED_TO_TRIGGER preserves bamboo failure summary') all use single-entry setups. The 'getQueuePositionForSuite returns correct position' test does enqueue two entries for the same planKey but never calls pollOnce() — it only checks queue position metadata. No test enqueues two WAITING_LOCAL entries for the same suitePlanKey and verifies that only the first one triggers. The guard at line 387 (if (oldestWaiting?.id != entry.id) return entry) is load-bearing: removing it would allow all WAITING_LOCAL entries to fire Bamboo triggers simultaneously. Severity Medium is accurate.
recommendedAction: Add a test that enqueues two entries for the same suitePlanKey, mocks getRunningBuilds to return empty and triggerBuild to succeed, calls testService.pollOnce(), and asserts only q-1 transitions to QUEUED_ON_BAMBOO while q-2 remains WAITING_LOCAL, with coVerify(exactly=1) on triggerBuild.

title: MonitorPanel.buildRunEntry: bambooUrl construction and filter buckets for most statuses untested
description: Lines 800-870 implement `buildRunEntry`, which maps every `QueueEntryStatus` to a `RunEntry` with a computed `bambooUrl`, `filterBucket`, `isTerminal`, and `status` label. Only `FAILED_TO_TRIGGER` and `WAITING_LOCAL` are tested. The following are untested: (a) `bambooUrl = "$bambooUrlBase/browse/$resultKey"` when both are non-blank (line 801-803); (b) `COMPLETED` → `filterBucket=MonitorFilter.COMPLETED`, `isTerminal=true`; (c) `FAILED` → `filterBucket=FAILED`, `isTerminal=true`, `errorMessage` forwarded; (d) `CANCELLED` → `filterBucket=FAILED` (not CANCELLED); (e) `RUNNING` and `QUEUED_ON_BAMBOO` → not terminal.
suggestedFix: Extend `MonitorPanelRunEntryTest` with parametrized or individual tests for each remaining status, using `bambooUrlBase = "https://bamboo.example.com"` and a non-blank `bambooResultKey`. Assert `bambooUrl`, `filterBucket`, `isTerminal`, and `status` label for each. In particular, assert that `CANCELLED` maps to `MonitorFilter.FAILED` (not a `CANCELLED` bucket) and that `COMPLETED` maps to `MonitorFilter.COMPLETED`.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. MonitorPanelRunEntryTest.kt contains only 2 tests: one for FAILED_TO_TRIGGER and one for WAITING_LOCAL (lines 38-63). The source in MonitorPanel.kt lines 808-872 implements distinct logic for COMPLETED (filterBucket=COMPLETED, isTerminal=true), FAILED (filterBucket=FAILED, isTerminal=true, errorMessage forwarded), CANCELLED (filterBucket=FAILED — not a CANCELLED bucket), RUNNING (isTerminal=false, filterBucket=QUEUED), and QUEUED_ON_BAMBOO (isTerminal=false). None of these paths are exercised by any test. The bambooUrl construction at lines 801-803 (non-blank resultKey + non-blank bambooUrlBase) is also untested. However, buildRunEntry is a pure deterministic mapping function with no external dependencies — a regression here would be immediately visible in the UI, and the logic itself is straightforward. Severity is Medium as claimed, but lowered to Low because the function is pure, the logic is not complex, and there is no indication of a latent bug; this is purely a coverage gap.
recommendedAction: Add parametrized tests in MonitorPanelRunEntryTest covering each remaining QueueEntryStatus branch, including the bambooUrl construction with non-blank resultKey and bambooUrlBase, and asserting that CANCELLED maps to MonitorFilter.FAILED.

title: TagBuilderService.replaceCurrentRepoTag with null featureBranchTag returns entries unchanged — not tested
description: Line 261 has an early-return guard: `if (context.featureBranchTag == null) return entries`. This path (the service could not detect a feature branch tag for the current repo) returns the baseline list untouched. It is not covered by any test in `TagBuilderServiceTest`. If the guard is accidentally removed or negated, every baseline entry for the current repo service would be overwritten with `null`.
suggestedFix: Add a unit test: construct a `CurrentRepoContext` with `featureBranchTag = null`, call `service.replaceCurrentRepoTag(entries, context)`, and assert the returned list is identical to the input list (`assertSame` or deep-equality) with no entry having `isCurrentRepo = true`.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. TagBuilderService.kt line 261 has the early-return guard 'if (context.featureBranchTag == null) return entries'. TagBuilderServiceTest.kt has two tests for replaceCurrentRepoTag: one with a matching service and non-null featureBranchTag (line 77), and one with a non-matching service name (line 101). Neither test constructs a CurrentRepoContext with featureBranchTag=null. The null-tag path is a distinct code branch and is not covered. However, the risk is low because: the function is pure with no side effects, a null featureBranchTag simply means tag detection failed (legitimate scenario), and the guard is a simple early-return. Severity is correctly Low.
recommendedAction: Add a unit test constructing CurrentRepoContext with featureBranchTag=null, calling replaceCurrentRepoTag, and asserting the returned list is referentially or deeply equal to the input with no entry having isCurrentRepo=true.

title: BaselineCacheService.invalidate for non-existent key (no-op early return) untested
description: Line 89 has an early return: `if (entries.remove(planKey) == null) return`. This `return` exits the suspend function before calling `persistSnapshot`, so no disk write occurs when the key did not exist. The scenario is not tested. If this guard is inadvertently removed (e.g. by collapsing the null check), a spurious file-write — overwriting the cache with a missing key still included — would occur on every call to `invalidate` for an unknown key.
suggestedFix: Add a test that calls `svc.invalidate("NON-EXISTENT")` on a freshly constructed empty cache, then asserts `svc.get("NON-EXISTENT") == null` (no crash) and that no `baseline-cache.json` file was created in the cache directory (i.e. `!cacheFile.exists()`), confirming the no-op early return fires correctly.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. BaselineCacheService.kt lines 87-92 implement the invalidate() function with an early return when entries.remove(planKey) == null (key not present). The existing test at BaselineCacheServiceTest.kt line 87-92 ('invalidate removes the entry') only tests the happy path where the key exists. No test calls invalidate() on a non-existent key. If the null check is removed, invalidate() would call persistSnapshot() with an unchanged map snapshot on every no-op call — a spurious but harmless disk write. The risk of regression is very low because the guard only prevents unnecessary disk I/O. Severity is correctly Low.
recommendedAction: Add a test calling svc.invalidate("NON-EXISTENT") on a freshly constructed empty cache, asserting no crash and that no baseline-cache.json file was written to the temp directory.

title: TagBuilderService.scoreAndRankRuns: require() precondition violations (maxWalk < targetParseable, non-positive targetParseable) untested
description: Lines 81-82 have two `require()` guards: `require(targetParseable > 0)` and `require(maxWalk >= targetParseable)`. No test exercises these guards. A caller passing `targetParseable=0` or `maxWalk < targetParseable` would throw an `IllegalArgumentException` at runtime without any test evidence that the contract is enforced. Callers currently always use defaults, but future call sites (e.g. a configurable baseline depth setting) could violate these preconditions silently.
evidence:
require(targetParseable > 0) { "targetParseable must be positive" }
require(maxWalk >= targetParseable) { "maxWalk ($maxWalk) must be >= targetParseable ($targetParseable)" }
suggestedFix: Add two tests using `assertThrows<IllegalArgumentException>` wrapped in `runTest`: one calling `service.scoreAndRankRuns("PROJ", targetParseable = 0)` and asserting the exception message contains "positive"; another calling `service.scoreAndRankRuns("PROJ", targetParseable = 5, maxWalk = 3)` and asserting the exception message contains "maxWalk".
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. TagBuilderService.kt lines 81-82 have two require() guards: require(targetParseable > 0) and require(maxWalk >= targetParseable). TagBuilderServiceTest.kt has no test that exercises either guard with a violating argument; all existing tests use valid parameter values (defaulting to targetParseable=10, maxWalk=10, or using explicit valid values like targetParseable=6, maxWalk=12 and targetParseable=3, maxWalk=10). The guards are already enforced at runtime by Kotlin's require() — violation throws IllegalArgumentException immediately. No latent bug is present; this is purely a coverage gap. Future call sites with user-configurable values (e.g. a settings field for baseline depth) could hit these guards unexpectedly. Severity Low is accurate since all current callers pass defaults or otherwise valid values, and the precondition is already enforced by the language construct.
recommendedAction: Add two tests using assertThrows<IllegalArgumentException> in runTest: one for targetParseable=0 asserting the message contains 'positive', and one for targetParseable=5, maxWalk=3 asserting the message contains 'maxWalk'.
